Okay, we're going to wrap now by going through this. Now we have a gross CTO of $35.70, right? Now, it still cost us the same to mail the letters, which knocks it down to $3.00, $7.00 per thousand, and we had to fill more orders. How much is it in order? $40? $40. What's 15 times 40? So we got $600 there, minus $600. That comes up to, is that right, $24.70? $23.70. So $23.70. Would you punch in $23.70? Mary, you're always subtracting $500. How'd you do my tax balance cost? That's the mailing cost, but now we have to subtract fulfillment. That's the $600. He's got to fulfill 15 sets of clubs at $40 a piece. So you punched in... $24.70. $24.00. Let's make it $2,500 bucks, okay? $2,500 profit $1,000 times $1,500. $3,700. $3,750,000. Now, all of you turkeys are worried about the wrong thing. Whenever I talk to someone, and you're all not turkeys, maybe like only 95% of you, but when I talk to people about making money, especially not people like in this room, I don't even do it anymore because, you know, they ask questions, well, who's going to fill out the income tax forms on that? You know? Really and truly. I wrote an ad for a guy that made him $30 million, and all his orders were coming in by phone, and this guy paid me $1,200,000 for writing this ad. You know, that's what my commission... But I wouldn't write another ad for him because he annoyed me so much with his voice. And the first time he really annoyed me, he says, Gary, this is Mark. I'm calling you. He said, I just want to tell you, our phones are ringing off the hook. It's night and day. He says, we don't know what to do here. Now, you created this problem. I expect your help in solving it, Gary. I said, I'll tell you what, you stupid son of a bitch. I create problems like this. I don't solve them. You solve them. Okay? Now, you're worried about the financing, aren't you? You're worried about how are we going to get the money to do this? Our cash flow. Guess where your minds are when you're thinking about that? Get this into your head. If success is $100,000, getting the profit, getting that number is $90,000. All of the rest of it is LMS. Write that down. LMS. And that stands for lesser mortal shit. Lesser mortals will do the rest of it. If you can do that part. Terry stands up and says, now let me tell you what I mean by that. I absolutely guarantee that if you make a mailing and get these kind of results and you can't get it financed, I can. I can introduce you to 100 people who will back up any valid promotion with integrity with good numbers. That's not what you should worry about. The hard part is to make that sale at a profit. There are all kinds of guys who are factors. You all know what a factor is? In case anybody doesn't, you may not know what a factor is. A factor is somebody who will lend you money on your receivables. If you can't figure out another way, you take all your credit card orders and all your post-dated checks and you give them to the factor and he immediately writes you a check for 70% of the value of that in return for him getting all of it when it comes due. The people who go give AIDS, people who have the HIV virus, a lot of money to get their insurance money when they die, those people are factors. That's one way you can get this done. Another thing is there are people all over this nation and all over the United States. All they want is somebody with good numbers. They'll back it. The money is not the crucial ingredient. I don't know about $2 million back in the package. I thought it was money. Yeah. They're a partnership company. So they suck wind for 90 days. My point here is we're not going to... What I'm going to teach you here is how to get the sale. And, yes? I'm trying to find out if I understood you properly. Did you say you can expect a one-third to one-half return? That was my guess. That was just my guess. You may not be that high. But you know what? You don't care what it is. You know what you care about? The arithmetic. Would you rather... I had a guy, his name is Melvin Powers. He's an idiot. I'll say it on tape. He owns Wilshire Books. He wrote this book on how to make a million dollars in mail order. And he didn't know how to make a million dollars in mail order. So he hired me to write the ad for him. I wrote the ad and I put the post-dated check factor in it. And he refused to run it. And he had me in his office and he said, let me tell you why I refuse to run this. And I've tried this or I've done that. And look at these. He kept all of his bad checks in the drawer right there beside his desk. And I said, Melvin, did the promotion make a profit when you did the arithmetic? He said, yeah, but look at those bad checks. I said, Melvin, if it made a profit, you don't want a drawer full of bad checks. You want a barn full, right? It's in the arithmetic. Within reason, who cares what the return percentage is? Who cares? The numbers don't include the return. Yes, they do. Yes, they do. We deducted them. Yes, we did. And so the thing that I want to emphasize over and over, and I do this more than anybody else in the business, when I work with somebody new or something, is I go after the arithmetic. I slam that home all the time. The arithmetic. Would you rather have, you know, Joe Carbo used to have people that would brag to him and they'd say, Joe, I have less than a 1% refund rate. You know what Joe would say? You don't sell hard enough. You know? You're not selling hard enough. Actually, you can probably arrange things so that you have practically zero refund rate. You know, you won't have a big, very big gross. Now, there's a point that you want to take it. But the thing that I want to remember, we're going to close this with, is the following. Oh, one other thing I want to tell you too. Seminars, I have learned, they never really start to the second day. They don't. It doesn't matter how good your speakers are, how fresh your material, or anything. The reason for that is that when people are attending a high priced multi-day seminar like this, nobody's really here the first day. You're still wondering if you left the oven on. Was it worth the money you shelled out to come here? What's Mary in the office doing to your filing, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera? Although my goal is to give everybody good information on the first day of a seminar, the main purpose of this first day is to fatigue you. It is. It is. I want you to be so tired when you go home tonight that you'll just go to bed. Your critical mind will switch off, and you will come in tomorrow, and you will see the difference in the room. I've noticed that all the time. It's like the room comes alive on the second day. The first day is basically just a stool softener. A mental stool softener. Okay? Now, but the two things that I want, probably sum up little three things, is you just saw me do this right in front of your face because I have done it so many times, but I had a good template. I didn't have to create it in a vacuum. You saw the reason I chose what I chose to work on is because years from now, when someone looks at this tape, I want to say, did you see the guy who did that for on the tape? He made $14 million doing that. That means I didn't want a particularly big challenge. Okay, so write these final notes down. Number one, shoot fish in a barrel. Now, you all know what that means. Number one, shoot fish in a barrel. It means unless you guys are brain dead beyond belief, you don't need any more challenges in your life, do you? Does anybody here need any more challenges? Shoot fish in a barrel. Go for the easy stuff. Number two, concentrate on making the sale. And you don't have to write this, but I want to tell you, when I say concentrate on making the sale, I mean that so strongly that you won't even allow anybody else to talk to you about the rest of it. Okay. All right. Uh, Terry, do we have the SRDS book? We just have a little bit of a video. Okay. Well, you just may want to take some notes here on stuff that they want to get. Because I'll tell you what I'm going to do. I'm going to answer all of those questions. Um, but at the same time in any given endeavor, when you're a novice at it, one of the problems is you don't know what to worry about. You know, you end up worrying about the wrong things. Um, I'll use a scuba diving analogy once more. Um, a lot of people, if they're thinking about taking up scuba diving, what they're going to worry about is getting eaten by a shark. And sometimes those people will read books on sharks and watch documentary movies and everything. And the real truth is it would be hard to get yourself eaten by a shark if you wanted to. If you wanted to commit suicide by having a shark eat you, you would have a tough road to hoe. And when you make documentaries, for example, about the great white, they have to go out and throw cow carcasses in the water and spread blood around and hang out for days and sometimes a couple weeks to get a shark to bite, you know, to bite someone. Um, if you're a scuba diver and you're going to die, you're going to die because you drowned and you run out of air. Or you're going to die because you ascend too fast and you embolize. So what I'm going to do here is I'm going to tell you, take the first maybe hour this morning and tell you the things that you really need to know, as opposed perhaps to the things that you want to know at this moment, alright? Um, in my direct mail letter, I have a lot of bullets of things that I promise to teach you and I'm going to teach you all of those things. However, if there is anything that I skip over before this session is finished, before the seminar is finished, it will be something technical related to production, which I'm going to summarize and give to you in a letter and an audio tape. I would, I will try not to have to do that. I want to summarize it all for you written form anyway. But what I want to make sure, absolutely sure that you get here are the, are, is the information that you can get nowhere else, okay? The things that make it work. Your question is exactly dead on right. The first thing. Let me tell you, let's suppose that you are starting from ground zero and this will be very helpful for you, those of you who are not starting from ground zero. Also, what is the very first thing that you should do? And maybe we'll just itemize these in steps and we'll end up with them. I hadn't planned on doing this, but you know, Halbert's one, two, three, four steps that you should do. The number one very first step you should do is buy a subscription to the SRDS mailing list book. The SRDS mailing list book, that stands for Standard Rate and Data Service. And before this seminar is over, Terry will give you the telephone number where you can call and order it. That book is about that thick and about that wide. We will have one here to show you. And it contains descriptions of thousands of mailing lists. Now, here's what it will tell you about those mailing lists. It will tell you the name of the list and how many names there are on the list and what the average unit of sale is and how the names were generated. Some names are generated by television offers. Some are generated by direct mail offers. Some are generated by magazine offers. Some are compiled lists of people who are not buyers but might be a logical prospect for something. The one I know about covers the United States and I believe Canada. They're probably, that's an interesting thought. I wonder if there is one for England and New Zealand and so forth. As a point of interest, mail order promotions that work in the United States work exponentially better outside the United States in other English speaking countries. So that's kind of interesting. Like the Code of Arms offer that we have just goes crazy outside the United States. So do many others. Now, here is what you should do when you get, now that comes out several times a year and it gets, and they send you update sheets. And the first thing that you should concern yourself if you are starting with zero is just going through that SRDS book and reading the descriptions of the list and doing the arithmetic that is in the list. And it gives you there to do. That is the very first thing. Yes, sir. You can get one at a local library. Sometimes you can get one from a list broker. Yeah, but yeah. Okay, we could ask them about that. As long as you and like so many other people direct marketing guys talk about the SRDS is why have it loaded the correct. I don't really maybe what I'm going to say next will answer that question. Okay. Now, actually what I just said, instead of making that step one, let's make that step two. Step one is you should rid yourself of all previous bad investments. Now, here's what I mean about that. I don't mean necessarily a real estate investment or something. A bad investment is enduring a marriage with a spouse you can't stand. It's enduring a business relationship with a partner who drives you crazy. It's going to work at a job that you find really boring. Now, practically you can't rid yourself of all your bad investments overnight or immediately, but the very worst bad investment in the world is a bad mindset. Now, it takes, it's very, very difficult to rid yourself of a bad mindset, but I think it is the single most important thing that you can do. And as it relates to this seminar, I will tell you one of the ways I'm specific bad mindset. Well, I'll ask someone, why did you come here? What do you want to learn to do? I want to learn to sell raincoats to people in Arizona that are fluorescent purple when it rains. Everybody's going to want them because they fluoresce when it rains and they won't get hit by cars. That man has an investment in that, even if he hasn't produced or purchased any raincoats. He has a mental investment, et cetera, et cetera. If you can wipe that slate clean, that is the best thing that you can do. In other words, rid yourself of preconceptions. I'm assuming now if you are emotionally in love with something you're doing, and that's fine. You know, like a book that teaches children to write or you have a passion for something, that's not what I'm talking about. But I'm talking about most of you are here because you won't want to learn how to write a sales letter that will make you rich. Step one is erase your preconceived notions about what to do. Step two is to get yourself a copy of the SRDS book. I would spend the money to get a subscription so that if it's a few hundred dollars, whatever, but sometimes if you're not in a position to do that, you can go to a library and get leaked through their copies, or you can probably finagle away to get used copies or whatever. But the main thing is get yourself the SRDS book and begin reading the descriptions of the list and start doing the arithmetic there. Now let me tell you what we want. We want to sell to people who have already proven they will buy something by mail. And we want there to be a lot of those people. We want them to have a high-passion index. We want them, that's what we want. Let's stop guessing. Do you understand what I'm saying? Let's stop fantasizing. Hey, I've got this great idea. A woman came up to me and I got this great idea. And if I did all the marketing, arranged for production and paid for all the advertising and everything else, she was going to split the profits with me. And her idea was a woman's purse that looked like a lobster trap. I could give you an example after example of that. Now as you go through the SRDS book, just read it blindly. Just read it and read it and read it and do the math and you will come upon information like this. It'll say something like, Rodale Press. Now they have a particular promotion out now that is a manual for people who want to have better sex. It's a big, thick book. And the control package was written by an excellent copywriter named Punkry. And it's a very legitimate, very authoritative book. And I just call it the sex book here. And it's a book that has held 6 million pieces of mail to sell that book last December. Just last December, okay? Now I don't know the numbers on that book or anything. I do know that John Carlton has been hired by them and has just completed a new piece which I know will become the control. I helped him with it. He and I help each other on most of the things. I say that you look at Rodale Press, sex book, et cetera, et cetera, and it's going to say something like, you know, recent buy, it'll say active or recent buyers. And I don't know what the number is, but let's pretend it's 325,000. And then it will say hotline buyers. And that means the people who have bought just now, this month, or sometimes a hotline is the last 60-day hotline, or the 90-day hotline. It's like how many they're selling a month. And let's say that that figure is 50,000. Okay? And let us say, for example, I don't know, I don't remember what the book sells for, let's say it's $39.95 or something. Let's say it's $40. Now, someone with a calculator, I want you to multiply 325,000 by 40. Tell me what that is. Big part? A million, 300,000? No, it's more than that. It's 13 million. Okay? What's 50,000 times 40? Two million? Okay. Now, you read this, and you do the math there, and you say, wow, these people have sold 325,000 copies of this manual on how to have better sex, and they're selling an average of 50,000 of these a month at 40 bucks. You do the math on that. Then you know what you do? You go to the next entry, and you read, then you go ahead and you read the next entry in the SRDS list book. And let's say that it is something different. Let's say that it is, I'd say Suarez has a, Ben Suarez has a, add out now on how to flatten your stomach. Okay? Flatten your tummy, we'll call it. And they'll tell you the name of its Suarez industry. Let's say that this thing, again, I don't remember what it sells for. Say it's 29.95 or something, and change. Say it goes for about $30. Okay? And let's say that he has just begun that promotion recently, which he has, and he sold 275,000 of them, and he's selling the 35,000 a month on the hotline. Somebody do the math on that. What's 275,000 times 30? Okay, 8,250,000. What's 35,000 times 30? Whatever, it's over a million, right? Okay. Now there are thousands of descriptions of mailing lists in the SRDS. The first thing you should do, drop your mindset, the second thing you should do is get the SRDS and start reading it. And you should just read it and read it and read it. And just read these list descriptions and do it with a calculator at your side. Okay. Now, by the time, now I want to tell you something about mailing lists. Because believe it or not, the sales letter is the single most, or is not the single most important thing. The mailing list is the very most important ingredient. You are not likely to die scuba diving because a shark eats you. The most likely reason is you will drown or embolize. The most likely reason, the number one reason that you will fail in an attempt to create a mail order business is because you do not have an audience that is made up of people who have demonstrated by writing a check or giving a credit card order or what have you that they are willing to buy by mail something. That's the first thing you want to know. The major reason that you are going to fail is you are going to be pioneering something that you think people should want. People don't buy what they should want. They buy what they want. Whatever. That's what it is. Okay. Now, for those of you who are complete novices, I'll tell you what makes a good mailing list. All of the factors. The first factor, we're going to describe a perfect prospect. Number one, he is a buyer. He's not someone who filled out a questionnaire and said, yes, I'm interested in XYZ. He's someone who wrote a check and sent it to somebody because he wanted to buy it. Buyer. And I'm going to put buyer by direct response. So that's the number one thing that he is a buyer. That proves that he is a real prospect for you. Okay. The second thing that we can use to gauge how high his passion index is. And I'll understand that concept of passion index is the unit of sale. Now, I'll ask you some obvious questions. Would you rather try and sell whatever it is that you're trying to sell to someone who says, yeah, I think I'm who said, who said or filled out a questionnaire. I might be interested in this or someone who bought something related to that. You'd rather try and market to the buyer, right? Now, the next thing is would you rather try and market what you are selling to someone who paid $10 to buy something related to what you're selling, like a $10 book, or someone who paid $300. Who's the better prospect? Unit of sale. And as a general rule, the higher the better. You know, I publish a pretty expensive newsletter and I can assure you that I get a better response from people who have paid $200 or $300 for other newsletters than people who have paid $10 for a vague plan on, you know, how to get rich or something. So everything else being equal, you want the unit of sale. You want, and that's the dollar amount, okay? Absolutely. Absolutely. Consider, I don't know the exact answer to this, but I know the answer in principle, how much more money does a golfer pay to indulge his hobby than a bowler? I mean, there's not even a comparison, is there? Okay. And so therefore, it's pretty difficult, if you have any skill at all, to miss marketing something to a golfer, a group of golfers, especially a group of golfers who have bought something by mail that promised to help them lower their score. So they have a higher, so that is a direct measurement of it. Now, let's suppose that this, let's just suppose it is the person has bought a book on how to pay better golf. Would you rather mail to that guy if you had something to sell or a guy who's bought 15 books? You want the guy who's bought 15 books, right? So that's frequency. Okay. Now, the next one is recency. Would you rather try and sell, what it is you have to sell, to a guy who bought a book on how to reduce his golf score last month or a guy who bought a book 10 years ago? Last month, obviously. So it's recency. Okay. Now, as you go through the SRDS book, you will begin reading all of this list information. You'll have a calculator at your side and you will do numbers. And what this will do is take a lot of nonsense out of your head. It also will take a great, this is the number one thing you can do. The number one thing you can do to ensure success at your endeavor is to become aware of markets, of the reality of markets. So many people sell what they think people should buy. Do not do that, sell people more of what they have already demonstrated they are willing to buy. Preferably, they have demonstrated that they are willing to buy it, willing to buy it by mail, willing to pay a lot of money, willing to buy it over and over, and bought it yesterday. There are many, many such lists, more than you would imagine. You look at these lists and you do the math on them. And I can tell you that will be a very exciting experience for you. What seems like dull reading of a reference book will make you come alive. If some of you, it will ruin your sleep patterns because you'll think, my God, you know, and you'll realize some little ad that you've seen over and over, or some letter you've gotten in the mail. Just how much this guy, I had a client in Santa Barbara who was running little ads, little classified ads on government jobs and how to get jobs as an airline hostess, et cetera, et cetera. And he hired me. I went up. This guy was making clearly a quarter million dollars a week on classified ads. The numbers that people make, unless you're an insider, almost defied the leaf. You can't really even describe this. Sometimes a little mental picture of it will help you. When Dennis Haslinger and I started the code of arms business, we strategically located in a little building across from the Bath, Ohio, Coast Office. And we told the Postmaster, who we later became good friends with and nominated him for Postmaster of the Year, that to really remember who we were because we were going to get a lot of mail, et cetera, et cetera. And the first month or two we were in business, he would come up and knock on the door and say, here's your three letters. I was able to give them to you today, you know, and stuff like that. And then we began mailing more and began more and more and more. And we got to the point, because we thought the Bath, Ohio postmark was important, that my brother-in-law was, who was a trucker with a semi truck, was going to Reuben Donnelly in Oak Park, Illinois, and getting two truck bulls of mail a week and bringing them in. And we got up to mailing one million letters a week in peak seasons, a million letters a week. Now, the responses differed a little bit, but we took in on many days and that company still does 20,000 checks a day. 20,000 checks a day. My mother and my father both knew I would never amount to anything. And I was renting a house on Singing Brook Drive in Maslin, Ohio for $750 a month, and my parents were outraged. And I said, believe me, it's okay. It really is okay. Well, they wouldn't. So I said to my dad, well, here's what happened with this money coming in. First, we started making the deposit, and then we hired a crew of women to help us make the deposit. We got up to like 30 or 40 women who did nothing but make the bank deposit. It's a full-time job to deposit 20,000 checks a day. Then the bank gave us the third floor of the First National Bank Building in Barberton, Ohio that did nothing but work on our account. Then the bank gave us a building, a whole building that did nothing but work on our account. Now, catch this. My father, I said, dad, it's okay. I can afford the 750 a month. It's really, honestly. And he couldn't believe it, so I said, I want to show you something. And I picked a Monday. And this is when we had moved from doing it ourselves to the bank was doing a spores on the third floor of the building. So I get my dad dressed up. We get dressed up and we go down on the Monday, which is our heaviest mail day. And we go in and there's a room like the table like this with employees sitting down there of the bank, and they're all counting checks and money and cash. And there's other people wheeling it in with a duffel bag, a cart with a duffel bag. And they're coming up to the cart with a duffel bag and they're, excuse me, stepping on my grief. They're dumping this down and my father's going like that. And he says, and I love this is my favorite memory of my father. And he said to the woman who is in charge, my God, he said that's a lot of checks to get. She said, that's just what we got today, Mr. Halber. And my father lost his ability to speak. I love it. Honestly, God, he lost his ability to speak. And he couldn't speak again until he had three double shots of whiskey. And we went home. We went home and my mom, who is still was very skeptical about this. She said, well, Cliff, you know, what's he done? He gets a drink, you know, and what is it? And he looks at my mom and says, you won't believe what this boy has done. And so my mother did not believe it. Well, I had planned a vacation to Florida then and I went on vacation. But when I came back, now I'm going to show my mom, right? So I get my mom all dressed up and I take her down to the bank. Unbeknownst to me, while I'm gone, they had moved our operation from the third floor of the bank to the building. And all there is there is an empty building with rubble and old telephone cable. And my mother knew it was all bullshit, you know. Really and truly, she thought it was kidding. But I say that because, I mean, it's hard for you to imagine what happens when one of these things explodes. And it really, really can happen. Dennis Haslinger and I were taking in the equivalent of today's money, about 360,000 bucks a day. And there are people who are doing that now. Phillips Publishing is the only client I have. I have two controls with them. They're the biggest newsletter publisher in the world. They do $160 million a year. They do about a million dollars every working day. You ought to see that. You ought to see that come in. And it all is contingent on the first thing is getting a market, going to a market that's right, and then writing the letter to that market. So the first thing you should do is drop your mindset. Second thing you should do is you should read the SRDS. And unless you are really should be working at McDonald's, by the time you finish reading this SRDS book and reading all these things, you're going to make a decision on what it is that you want to sell. That is a lot more intelligent than you would have made before you read this book, right? Now, if you do not have a product already and you're looking around for something to sell, don't choose anything else except information. You want to sell paper and ink, information on a CD-ROM, a videotape, an audio tape. You want to sell seminars or what have you. The reason for that is that this is a nation that is how-to-hungry. This nation is starving for expertise, and it is only increasing more and more. People are finding out that what they learned in school isn't as useful as the promise was. How many of you studied Latin in school? How helpful is that to you, all the Latin you learned? How many of you studied trigonometry in school? Keep your hands up if you use it every day. On and on and on, they have taken thousands of hours of our minds and they have crammed into it absolutely useless information. What they haven't done is they didn't cram very much good information in there. What we find is we find out all of a sudden we wake up and we're 40 years old and we don't know where the bathroom is. We might know how to do a conjunction of a Latin verb, but we can't find the men's room. We find someone in an area of expertise and we pay them to come to a seminar like this to learn it. One of the reasons you should sell information is because that's what people want to buy. The other thing is because it has a high market. When someone buys my newsletter, they're not paying $200 a year for the paper and ink. When you buy a videotape to lower your golf score, you're not paying for the tape in there. You're paying for that information, that expertise. We are now more than ever before a nation that wants to buy software. And by software, I mean not just computer software. What I am selling you today is software, isn't it? I'm selling you my experience and my expertise. All right. Now, we now have step one. Drop that stupid mindset. Step two, read the SRDS book. And step three is this is three. Choose a mailing list category or mailing list to work. Now, unless you have completely missed the point here, you're going to be looking for a big, hot list of names who have recently and frequently paid a lot of money for something, aren't you? And when you find that list, and you will find hundreds and hundreds of them, here's what you do next. You all read the newsletter about how I created a product, you know, right in the Miami library, right? You go, okay. And I've written another newsletter about that. Here's what you should do. Now, don't fault me on this because those of you who haven't done it, I'm going to, because I know where people mess up. Here's what you should do. You should go to a bookstore or go to an audio tape store or go to a video store and buy that material related that that list would want. Buy, and when I say buy it, like buy one or two of these existing products, right? Got it? That is buy product. Now, let's suppose that you have found that this is a golf list of people who have bought videos on how to lower their golf score, right? And you go to the bookstore and you find a book that's really good on how to lower your golf score, not the one they sold, and someone else has written it, all right? Now, when I say buy a product, I said buy one product. Now, we have a mailing list and we have one product, right? It's not ours, but we have one, don't we? Now, the next thing that you do, we're up to step four, is order 5,000 names of that, of the mailing list that you have chosen. Get them on peel and stick labels. Now, actually I made that four, let's make that five, because four should be write a sales letter about that book. But we're going to, that's what this seminar is about, but four is to write a sales letter about the book, okay? The book that you don't necessarily own the rights to, but let me explain something to you. When you buy that book, guess who owns it? You do. After you buy that book, you can sell that book to me. Can you not? You know, if you had a book that was written by Claude Hopkins and you paid 20 bucks for it and I wanted to buy it from you for 100, would it be legal for you to sell it to me? Of course it would, okay? So you own the book. Now, you've written the sales letter and you've ordered 5,000, you've written the sales letter and had it typed and printed. You order 5,000 names on peel and stick labels, okay? That's five, right? Now, you get envelopes and you put those peel and stick labels, you take 1,000 of them off and put them on there. You put 1,000 stamps on the envelopes and you take the letter that you have written to sell this book, of which you have only one, okay? And you have it typed and you have it printed and you make 1,000 copies of it. And then the next step is to mail 1,000 letters. Okay? Now, right there is where people who aren't in business shed are going to go wrong because you're going to start thinking more on Fox. How am I going to make any money doing that? I've only got one book. I've only got one book, you know. The book sells for $39 and it's cost to me 500 bucks to mail these. Well, shit, even if I sell it for $43 and get it, it just won't work. You are not trying to make a profit. You know what you're trying to do? You're trying to buy information, right? You're trying to buy information. Now, let's talk about the results of your mailing because the results are going to fall into three possibilities from these 1,000 pieces of mail. Zippo. Nobody wants to buy the book or only two or three people want to buy it or the videotape or what have you, right? You want to get a book and a videotape and an audio tape and put them off together and make that your package. But your results just suck. Let me tell you what to do if your results suck. You say, well, gee, I just love this book. I'm going to keep trying to sell it over and over and over like this. That's what people do come to my seminars. What do you sell? I sell insurance against a plague of frogs. So yeah, how long you've been trying to do that? Forty-eight years. What do you want me to help you? I want you to help me sell them. I haven't been successful. If it doesn't work, drop it. You're 500 bucks down the tube. Slightly less than it costs you for your four years in college, right? The other end of the scale is that it works really, really well. Let's just say that it pulls 7%. Some things do. We made a thousand letters. What's 7%? How many orders? 70. 70. Now, this book, we charged for it $39.95 plus $4 shipping and handling. That's 44 bucks, right? And we sold 70. How much says 70 times 44? Isn't it? $3,080. What is, okay, now, what is that number? Contribution to overhead. Okay, now, what are we going to do with these 70 orders when you get them? No, no, no. You could do that, actually. You could do that. That's not such a bad idea. Actually, but what you're going to do is you're going to send the money back. Now, there's going to be somebody here, well, that's a lot of money, student, back. Oh, it's $3,000, student, back. Bob here, what are you saying? Honest to God, it's the way people think. Send the money back. Who cares about the $3,000? Or go out and buy the books and send them out. We haven't made any money, have we? We haven't made any money. But we have just learned something awfully, awfully valuable, haven't we? So, now, what we're going to do is we're going to take that golf book and we're going to extract all the good information out of it, aren't we? And we're going to go to the library and we're going to buy some more golf books. And then we're going to buy some video tapes, et cetera, et cetera. And then, then, we are going to sit down and create our own superior product, right? But, no, we did it because we identified a hot, reachable market and then we tested that. And now, you see the letter, we've discovered that the people will respond to a certain appeal, this group of people. Now, we go out and we create our own product. Only now do we invest the time in doing that. You pay somebody else to do that or you can do it yourself or what have you, but only now do you do that. Now, let's suppose that we have created the product. Oh, good point, good point. The other outcome is in the middle from zippo or tragic results to great results to kind of okay results. Now, here's what kind of okay would mean. If you're selling a product that has just for the sake of a $50 contribution to overhead after cost of fulfillment, you need 10 orders just to pay for your advertising cost. So, let's say that you don't get 10 orders, but you do get 15, okay? And so, your 15 times 50, actually that's pretty good, is 750. You subtract your 500 and that's $250 per thousand names mail. But if you get anything close to breakeven, break even or over-break even, then you have to make a judgment call. Are you going to pay more attention to this? And what a lot of people don't understand is that in today's world, how hard it is to get any kind of winner without the kind of instruction you're getting now. So, if you have something that wins or almost wins or breaks even or at least doesn't lose or at least doesn't lose a lot, then now we're going to get, I'm going to tell you what to do with that during the rest of the seminar.
